{{Short description|8th anthology of Japanese waka poetry (compiled 1201)}} {{italic title}} The {{nihongo|'''''Shin Kokin Wakashū'''''|新古今和歌集||"New Collection of Poems Ancient and Modern"}}, also known in abbreviated form as the {{nihongo|'''''Shin Kokinshū'''''|新古今集}} or even conversationally as the '''Shin Kokin''', is the eighth [[Nijūichidaishū|imperial anthology]] of [[waka (poetry)|waka]] poetry compiled by the Japanese court, beginning with the ''[[Kokin Wakashū]]'' circa 905 and ending with the ''[[Shinshokukokin Wakashū]]'' circa 1439. The name can be literally translated as "New Collection of Ancient and Modern Poems"Keene 192 and bears an intentional resemblance to that of the first anthology. Together with the ''[[Man'yōshū]]'' and the ''[[Kokin Wakashū|Kokinshū]]'', the ''Shin Kokinshū'' is widely considered to be one of the three most influential poetic anthologies in Japanese literary history. It was commissioned in 1201 by the [[:en:Daijō-tennō|retired emperor]] [[Emperor Go-Toba|Go-Toba]] (r. 1183–1198), who established a new Bureau of Poetry at his Nijō palace with eleven Fellows,Brower 8 headed by [[Fujiwara no Yoshitsune]],Kodansha ''Fujiwara no Yoshitsune'' for the purpose of conducting poetry contests and compiling the anthology. Despite its emphasis on contemporary poets, the ''Shin Kokinshū'' covered a broader range of poetic ages than the ''Kokinshū'', including ancient poems that the editors of the first anthology had deliberately excluded.Cook Introduction It was officially presented in 1205, on the 300th anniversary of the Completion tier 2]] (1162–1241), [[Fujiwara no Ariie]] (1155–1216), [[Fujiwara no Ietaka]] (1158–1237), [[Jakuren]] (c. 1139–1202), [[Minamoto no Michitomo]] (1171–1237) and [[:en:Asukai Masatsune|Asukai Masatsune]] (1170–1221). The anthology was also given a preface in Japanese prose by [[Fujiwara no Yoshitsune]] and a preface in Chinese—the scholarly language of the Court—by [[Fujiwara no Chikatsune]], in a manner reminiscent of the ''Kokinshū''. ==Significance== The significance of the ''Shin Kokinshū'' lies prominently in the technical expertise of the compilers, their novel and extensive use of the [[honkadori]] literary technique, and the effect that each of these elements had on Japanese poetry after its publication. Even though the ''Kokinshū'' is famous in part for its organization throughout the anthology, as each poem is generally given as a lead-in to the next, the ''Shin Kokinshū'' goes above and beyond the standard created by the original collection. The ''Kokinshū'' editors used linking poems as a general guide, but the ''Shin Kokinshū'' editors created "an anthology that may be read from beginning to end as a single long structure divided into books".Konishi 68 For example, in the sections on the topic of Spring, the editors pieced together a detailed representation in poetic form of the advancement of spring and the passage of time, using similar words and expressions to link each poem to the next.Konishi 74 In the section on travel poems, the progression is from ancient poets and styles to modern ones,Konishi 91 and as is common in Japanese waka anthologies, the sections on Love are arranged to show the stages of an affair from first love to bitter parting. This kind of detailed manipulation resulted in an anthology that did not necessarily contain all of the best works of the day. As Fujiwara no Teika complained, Go-Toba’s insistence on including the works of old, obscure or even unaccomplished composers in the anthology in order to maintain a11ropriate links to those poems that were worthwhile made the honor of having forty-six of his own poems included in the anthology less satisfactory.Brower 21 Individual egos aside, the result was a composition that not only spanned centuries of Japanese literary tradition and evolving literary styles but also provided a veritable textbook on what well and poorly written poems looked like. The elaborate linking format developed by the editors was also picked up and carried forward with the development of the [[renga]] or "linked verse" form, in which poets wrote a series of verses together in turns by continuing the image of the previous verse and introducing something new for the next poet to work with. Renga also made frequent use of the honkadori technique, since each poet had only a short phrase to work with and the ability to use allusions to prior, complete poems was an important one. The term "Honkadori" refers to the practice of "allusive variation," and can be literally translated as "taking from an original poem".Kodansha ''Honkadori'' Even though allusions to older poems were common in the poetic discourse of the day, following the 11th century and prior to Fujiwara no Teika’s experimentation with honkadori, it was frowned upon to make obvious borrowings from past writers.Bialock 182 However, that changed significantly with the publication of the ''Shin Kokinshū''. Instead of mimicking only the horizontal flow of the ''Kokinshū'', the poems in the New Collection also make vertical links to the poetic traditions of the past,Bialock 198 and by borrowing from specific poems and not simply from stock phrases, the authors and editors of the poems in the'' Shin Kokinshū'' were able to step away from overused and more clearly unoriginal topics that ancient poems had popularized.Bialock 208 The following example compares one of Teika’s own poems in the ''Shin Kokinshū'' to its honka, or original poem, in the ''Kokinshū''. {{Verse translation| {{lang|ja-Latn|Samushiro ya matsu yo no aki no kaze fukete tsuki wo katashiku uji no hashihime}} | How cold! waiting out the autumn’s weary night deepening as the wind blows she spreads out the moon’s light the Princess of Uji Bridge.|attr2=Teika SKKS 4:420Bialock 205}} {{Verse translation| {{lang|ja-Latn|Samushiro ni koromo katashiki koyoi mo ya ware wo matsuramu uji no hashihime}} | On the cold mat spreading out her gown, this night too will she wait for me— the Princess of Uji Bridge?|attr2=KKS 14:684Bialock 205}} Although the poems are written on the same subject, with the newer one drawing directly from the older, Fujiwara no Teika’s interpretation both modernizes the poem and provides it with greater subtlety. It also accomplishes a connection between the ''Kokinshū'' and the ''Shin Kokinshū''. ==Structure== The structure of the ''Shin Kokinshū'' echoes that of the ''Kokinshū'' in many ways, but it also shows the influence of the intervening imperial anthologies. As can be seen in the table below, the ''Shin Kokinshū'' omits certain books from the original anthology, and includes others on poetic topics that gained prominence only after the publication of the ''Kokinshū''. {| class="wikitable" |- ! Topic !colspan="2" align="center"| Kokinshū !colspan="2" align="center"| Shin Kokinshū |- !rowspan="4" valign="middle"| Seasons | 1-2 || Spring |align="right"|1-2||Spring |- | 3 || Summer |align="right"|3||Summer |- | 4-5 || Autumn |align="right"|4-5||Autumn |- | 6 || Winter |align="right"|6||Winter |- !rowspan="4"|   | 7 || Congratulations |align="right"| 7 || Congratulations |- | 8 || Partings |align="right"| 8 || Laments |- | 9 || Travel |align="right"| 9 || Partings |- | 10 || Acrostics |align="right"| 10 || Travel |- ! Love | 11-15 || Love |align="right"| 11-15 || Love |- !rowspan="4" valign="middle"| Miscellany | 16 || Laments !rowspan="2" valign="middle"| 16-18 !rowspan="2" valign="middle"| Miscellaneous |- | 17-18 || Miscellaneous |- | 19 || Miscellaneous Forms |align="right"| 19 || Shinto Poems |- |valign="top"| 20 | Traditional Poems
from the Bureau of Song |valign="top" align="right"| 20 |valign="top"| Buddhist Poems |} The 20 books of the Shin Kokinshū contain nearly 2,000 [[waka (poetry)|waka]], with the number varying depending on the edition, as Go-Toba continued to edit the anthology extensively even after his exile to the island of Oki.Cook Editorial Note Each poem is introduced with information regarding the occasion for which it was composed (if that information was available) and in most cases an author is also listed. Major contemporary poetic contributors to the Shin Kokinshū include [[Saigyō Hōshi|Saigyō]] with 94 poems; [[Jien]] with 92; Fujiwara no Yoshitsune with 79; [[Fujiwara no Shunzei]] with 72; [[Princess Shikishi]] with 49; Fujiwara no Teika with 46; Fujiwara no Ietaka with 43; Jakuren with 35; and Go-Toba with 33. ==See also== * [[:en:1205 in poetry|1205 in poetry]] * [[List of Japanese poetry anthologies]] * [[Japanese poetry]] * [[:en:13th century in poetry|13th century in poetry]] * [[Honkadori]] ==Notes== {{reflist}} ==References== *{{citation|last = Bialock|first = David T.|year = 1994|title = Voice, Text, and the Question of Poetic Borrowing in Late Classical Japanese Poetry|periodical = [[Harvard Journal of Asiatic Studies]]|volume = 54|pages = 181–231|jstor =2719391|doi = 10.2307/2719391|issue = 1|publisher = Harvard-Yenching Institute }} *{{citation|last = Brower|first = Robert H.|year = 1972|title = 'Ex-Emperor Go-Toba's Secret Teachings': Go-Toba no in Gokuden|periodical = [[Harvard Journal of Asiatic Studies]]|volume = 32|pages = 5–70|doi = 10.2307/2718867|jstor = 2718867|publisher = Harvard-Yenching Institute|url = https://dl.dropboxusercontent.com/u/182368464/japanese-literature/1972-brower-exemperorgotobassecretteachings.pdf|url-status = dead|archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20160303193542/https://dl.dropboxusercontent.com/u/182368464/japanese-literature/1972-brower-exemperorgotobassecretteachings.pdf|archive-date = 2016-03-03}} *{{citation|last = Cook|first = Lewis|date = August 1999 |at=Introduction|title = Shinkokinshū|periodical = Japanese Text Initiative|url = http://etext.virginia.edu/japanese/shinkokinshu/AnoShin.intro.html}} *{{citation|last = Cook|first = Lewis|date = August 1999 |at=Editorial Note|title = Shinkokinshū|periodical = Japanese Text Initiative|url = http://etext.virginia.edu/japanese/shinkokinshu/AnoShin.ednote.html}} *{{citation|last=Keene|first=Donald|author-link=Donald Keene|year=1955|title=Anthology of Japanese Literature|place= New York, NY|publisher= Grove Press}} *{{citation|last1 = Konishi|first1 = Jun'ichi|year = 1958 |last2 = Trans. Robert H. Brower and Earl Miner|title = Association and Progression: Principles of Integration in Anthologies and Sequences of Japanese Court Poetry, A.D. 900-1350|periodical = [[Harvard Journal of Asiatic Studies]]|volume = 21|pages = 67–127|doi = 10.2307/2718620|jstor = 2718620|author3 = Miner, Earl|publisher = Harvard-Yenching Institute|first2 = Robert H.}} *"Honkadori." ''Kodansha Encyclopedia of Japan.'' 18 December 2007 *"Fujiwara no Yoshitsune." ''Kodansha Encyclopedia of Japan.'' 18 December 2007 *"Shin Kokinshū." ''Kodansha Encyclopedia of Japan.'' 18 December 2007 {{Imperial Waka Anthologies}} {{Authority control}} {{draft categories| [[Category: of the ''Kokinshū''. ==Editors of the anthology== Although Go-Toba retained veto power over the poems included in the anthology as well as the order in which they were presented,Kodansha ''Shin Kokinshū'' he assigned the task of compilation to six of the Fellows of the Bureau of Poetry.Brower 8 These were [[Fujiwara no Teika]] [[Category:Japanese poetry anthologies]] [[Category:1200s in Japan]] [[Category:Emperor Go-Toba]] [[Category:Buddhist poetry]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]] }} [[sn:Shin Kokin Wakashū]] [[en:Shin Kokin Wakashū]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[Category:pages with valid en interwikis]] [[ar:شين كوكين واكاشو]] {{Commons category|Shin Kokin Wakashū}} [[de:Shinkokin-wakashū]] [[fa:شین کوکین واکاشو]] [[fr:Shin kokin wakashū]] [[it:Shin Kokin Wakashū]] [[ja:新古今和歌集]] [[ko:신고금 와카집]] [[ru:Син кокин вака-сю]] [[uk:Сін кокін вака-сю]] [[zh:新古今和歌集]]

from the Bureau of Song |valign="top" align="right"| 20 |valign="top"| Buddhist Poems |} The 20 books of the Shin Kokinshū contain nearly 2,000 [[waka (poetry)|waka]], with the number varying depending on the edition, as Go-Toba continued to edit the anthology extensively even after his exile to the island of Oki.Cook Editorial Note Each poem is introduced with information regarding the occasion for which it was composed (if that information was available) and in most cases an author is also listed. Fujiwara no Teika with 46; Fujiwara no Ietaka with 43; Jakuren with 35; and Go-Toba with 33. ==See also== * [[:en:1205 in poetry|1205 in poetry]] * [[List of Japanese poetry anthologies]] * [[Japanese poetry]] * [[:en:13th century in poetry|13th century in poetry]] * [[Honkadori]] ==Notes== {{reflist}} ==References== *{{citation|last = Bialock|first = David T.|year = 1994|title = Voice, Text, and the Question of Poetic Borrowing in Late Classical Japanese Poetry|periodical = [[Harvard Journal of Asiatic Studies]]|volume = 54|pages = 181–231|jstor =2719391|doi = 10.2307/2719391|issue = 1|publisher = Harvard-Yenching Institute }} *{{citation|last = Brower|first = Robert H.|year = 1972|title = 'Ex-Emperor Go-Toba's Secret Teachings': Go-Toba no in Gokuden|periodical = [[Harvard Journal of Asiatic Studies]]|volume = 32|pages = 5–70|doi = 10.2307/2718867|jstor = 2718867|publisher = Harvard-Yenching Institute|url = https://dl.dropboxusercontent.com/u/182368464/japanese-literature/1972-brower-exemperorgotobassecretteachings.pdf|url-status = dead|archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20160303193542/https://dl.dropboxusercontent.com/u/182368464/japanese-literature/1972-brower-exemperorgotobassecretteachings.pdf|archive-date = 2016-03-03}} *{{citation|last = Cook|first = Lewis|date = August 1999 |at=Introduction|title = Shinkokinshū|periodical = Japanese Text Initiative|url = http://etext.virginia.edu/japanese/shinkokinshu/AnoShin.intro.html}} *{{citation|last = Cook|first = Lewis|date = August 1999 |at=Editorial Note|title = Shinkokinshū|periodical = Japanese Text Initiative|url = http://etext.virginia.edu/japanese/shinkokinshu/AnoShin.ednote.html}} *{{citation|last=Keene|first=Donald|author-link=Donald Keene|year=1955|title=Anthology of Japanese Literature|place= New York, NY|publisher= Grove Press}} *{{citation|last1 = Konishi|first1 = Jun'ichi|year = 1958 |last2 = Trans. {{Short description|8th anthology of Japanese waka poetry (compiled 1201)}} {{italic title}} The {{nihongo|'''''Shin Kokin Wakashū'''''|新古今和歌集||"New Collection of Poems Ancient and Modern"}}, also known in abbreviated form as the {{nihongo|'''''Shin Kokinshū'''''|新古今集}} or even conversationally as the '''Shin Kokin''', is the eighth [[Nijūichidaishū|imperial anthology]] of [[waka (poetry)|waka]] poetry compiled by the Japanese court, beginning with the ''[[Kokin Wakashū]]'' circa 905 and ending with the ''[[Shinshokukokin Wakashū]]'' circa 1439. The name can be literally translated as "New Collection of Ancient and Modern Poems"Keene 192 and bears an intentional resemblance to that of the first anthology. Together with the ''[[Man'yōshū]]'' and the ''[[Kokin Wakashū|Kokinshū]]'', the ''Shin Kokinshū'' is widely considered to be one of the three most influential poetic anthologies in Japanese literary history. It was commissioned in 1201 by the [[:en:Daijō-tennō|retired emperor]] [[:en:Emperor Go-Toba|Go-Toba]] (r. 1183–1198), who established a new Bureau of Poetry at his Nijō palace with eleven Fellows,Brower 8 headed by [[Fujiwara no Yoshitsune]],Kodansha ''Fujiwara no Yoshitsune'' for the purpose of conducting poetry contests and compiling the anthology. Despite its emphasis on contemporary poets, the ''Shin Kokinshū'' covered a broader range of poetic ages than the ''Kokinshū'', including ancient poems that the editors of the first anthology had deliberately excluded.Cook Introduction It was officially presented in 1205, on the 300th anniversary of the Completion tier 2]] (1162–1241), [[:en:Fujiwara no Ariie|Fujiwara no Ariie]] (1155–1216), [[Fujiwara no Ietaka]] (1158–1237), [[Jakuren]] (c. 1139–1202), [[Minamoto no Michitomo]] (1171–1237) and [[:en:Asukai Masatsune|Asukai Masatsune]] (1170–1221). The anthology was also given a preface in Japanese prose by [[Fujiwara no Yoshitsune]] and a preface in Chinese—the scholarly language of the Court—by [[Fujiwara no Chikatsune]], in a manner reminiscent of the ''Kokinshū''. ==Significance== The significance of the ''Shin Kokinshū'' lies prominently in the technical expertise of the compilers, their novel and extensive use of the [[honkadori]] literary technique, and the effect that each of these elements had on Japanese poetry after its publication. Even though the ''Kokinshū'' is famous in part for its organization throughout the anthology, as each poem is generally given as a lead-in to the next, the ''Shin Kokinshū'' goes above and beyond the standard created by the original collection. The ''Kokinshū'' editors used linking poems as a general guide, but the ''Shin Kokinshū'' editors created "an anthology that may be read from beginning to end as a single long structure divided into books".Konishi 68 For example, in the sections on the topic of Spring, the editors pieced together a detailed representation in poetic form of the advancement of spring and the passage of time, using similar words and expressions to link each poem to the next.Konishi 74 In the section on travel poems, the progression is from ancient poets and styles to modern ones,Konishi 91 and as is common in Japanese waka anthologies, the sections on Love are arranged to show the stages of an affair from first love to bitter parting. This kind of detailed manipulation resulted in an anthology that did not necessarily contain all of the best works of the day. As Fujiwara no Teika complained, Go-Toba’s insistence on including the works of old, obscure or even unaccomplished composers in the anthology in order to maintain a11ropriate links to those poems that were worthwhile made the honor of having forty-six of his own poems included in the anthology less satisfactory.Brower 21 Individual egos aside, the result was a composition that not only spanned centuries of Japanese literary tradition and evolving literary styles but also provided a veritable textbook on what well and poorly written poems looked like. The elaborate linking format developed by the editors was also picked up and carried forward with the development of the [[renga]] or "linked verse" form, in which poets wrote a series of verses together in turns by continuing the image of the previous verse and introducing something new for the next poet to work with. Renga also made frequent use of the honkadori technique, since each poet had only a short phrase to work with and the ability to use allusions to prior, complete poems was an important one. The term "Honkadori" refers to the practice of "allusive variation," and can be literally translated as "taking from an original poem".Kodansha ''Honkadori'' Even though allusions to older poems were common in the poetic discourse of the day, following the 11th century and prior to Fujiwara no Teika’s experimentation with honkadori, it was frowned upon to make obvious borrowings from past writers.Bialock 182 However, that changed significantly with the publication of the ''Shin Kokinshū''. Instead of mimicking only the horizontal flow of the ''Kokinshū'', the poems in the New Collection also make vertical links to the poetic traditions of the past,Bialock 198 and by borrowing from specific poems and not simply from stock phrases, the authors and editors of the poems in the'' Shin Kokinshū'' were able to step away from overused and more clearly unoriginal topics that ancient poems had popularized.Bialock 208 The following example compares one of Teika’s own poems in the ''Shin Kokinshū'' to its honka, or original poem, in the ''Kokinshū''. {{Verse translation| {{lang|ja-Latn|Samushiro ya matsu yo no aki no kaze fukete tsuki wo katashiku uji no hashihime}} | How cold! waiting out the autumn’s weary night deepening as the wind blows she spreads out the moon’s light the Princess of Uji Bridge.|attr2=Teika SKKS 4:420Bialock 205}} {{Verse translation| {{lang|ja-Latn|Samushiro ni koromo katashiki koyoi mo ya ware wo matsuramu uji no hashihime}} | On the cold mat spreading out her gown, this night too will she wait for me— the Princess of Uji Bridge?|attr2=KKS 14:684Bialock 205}} Although the poems are written on the same subject, with the newer one drawing directly from the older, Fujiwara no Teika’s interpretation both modernizes the poem and provides it with greater subtlety. It also accomplishes a connection between the ''Kokinshū'' and the ''Shin Kokinshū''. ==Structure== The structure of the ''Shin Kokinshū'' echoes that of the ''Kokinshū'' in many ways, but it also shows the influence of the intervening imperial anthologies. As can be seen in the table below, the ''Shin Kokinshū'' omits certain books from the original anthology, and includes others on poetic topics that gained prominence only after the publication of the ''Kokinshū''. {| class="wikitable" |- ! Topic !colspan="2" align="center"| Kokinshū !colspan="2" align="center"| Shin Kokinshū |- !rowspan="4" valign="middle"| Seasons | 1-2 || Spring |align="right"|1-2||Spring |- | 3 || Summer |align="right"|3||Summer |- | 4-5 || Autumn |align="right"|4-5||Autumn |- | 6 || Winter |align="right"|6||Winter |- !rowspan="4"|   | 7 || Congratulations |align="right"| 7 || Congratulations |- | 8 || Partings |align="right"| 8 || Laments |- | 9 || Travel |align="right"| 9 || Partings |- | 10 || Acrostics |align="right"| 10 || Travel |- ! Love | 11-15 || Love |align="right"| 11-15 || Love |- !rowspan="4" valign="middle"| Miscellany | 16 || Laments !rowspan="2" valign="middle"| 16-18 !rowspan="2" valign="middle"| Miscellaneous |- | 17-18 || Miscellaneous |- | 19 || Miscellaneous Forms |align="right"| 19 || Shinto Poems |- |valign="top"| 20 | Traditional Poems
from the Bureau of Song |valign="top" align="right"| 20 |valign="top"| Buddhist Poems |} The 20 books of the Shin Kokinshū contain nearly 2,000 [[waka (poetry)|waka]], with the number varying depending on the edition, as Go-Toba continued to edit the anthology extensively even after his exile to the island of Oki.Cook Editorial Note Each poem is introduced with information regarding the occasion for which it was composed (if that information was available) and in most cases an author is also listed. Major contemporary poetic contributors to the Shin Kokinshū include [[Saigyō Hōshi|Saigyō]] with 94 poems; [[Jien]] with 92; Fujiwara no Yoshitsune with 79; [[Fujiwara no Shunzei]] with 72; [[Princess Shikishi]] with 49; Fujiwara no Teika with 46; Fujiwara no Ietaka with 43; Jakuren with 35; and Go-Toba with 33. ==See also== * [[:en:1205 in poetry|1205 in poetry]] * [[List of Japanese poetry anthologies]] * [[Japanese poetry]] * [[:en:13th century in poetry|13th century in poetry]] * [[Honkadori]] ==Notes== {{reflist}} ==References== *{{citation|last = Bialock|first = David T.|year = 1994|title = Voice, Text, and the Question of Poetic Borrowing in Late Classical Japanese Poetry|periodical = [[Harvard Journal of Asiatic Studies]]|volume = 54|pages = 181–231|jstor =2719391|doi = 10.2307/2719391|issue = 1|publisher = Harvard-Yenching Institute }} *{{citation|last = Brower|first = Robert H.|year = 1972|title = 'Ex-Emperor Go-Toba's Secret Teachings': Go-Toba no in Gokuden|periodical = [[Harvard Journal of Asiatic Studies]]|volume = 32|pages = 5–70|doi = 10.2307/2718867|jstor = 2718867|publisher = Harvard-Yenching Institute|url = https://dl.dropboxusercontent.com/u/182368464/japanese-literature/1972-brower-exemperorgotobassecretteachings.pdf|url-status = dead|archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20160303193542/https://dl.dropboxusercontent.com/u/182368464/japanese-literature/1972-brower-exemperorgotobassecretteachings.pdf|archive-date = 2016-03-03}} *{{citation|last = Cook|first = Lewis|date = August 1999 |at=Introduction|title = Shinkokinshū|periodical = Japanese Text Initiative|url = http://etext.virginia.edu/japanese/shinkokinshu/AnoShin.intro.html}} *{{citation|last = Cook|first = Lewis|date = August 1999 |at=Editorial Note|title = Shinkokinshū|periodical = Japanese Text Initiative|url = http://etext.virginia.edu/japanese/shinkokinshu/AnoShin.ednote.html}} *{{citation|last=Keene|first=Donald|author-link=Donald Keene|year=1955|title=Anthology of Japanese Literature|place= New York, NY|publisher= Grove Press}} *{{citation|last1 = Konishi|first1 = Jun'ichi|year = 1958 |last2 = Trans. Robert H. Brower and Earl Miner|title = Association and Progression: Principles of Integration in Anthologies and Sequences of Japanese Court Poetry, A.D. 900-1350|periodical = [[Harvard Journal of Asiatic Studies]]|volume = 21|pages = 67–127|doi = 10.2307/2718620|jstor = 2718620|author3 = Miner, Earl|publisher = Harvard-Yenching Institute|first2 = Robert H.}} *"Honkadori." ''Kodansha Encyclopedia of Japan.'' 18 December 2007 *"Fujiwara no Yoshitsune." ''Kodansha Encyclopedia of Japan.'' 18 December 2007 *"Shin Kokinshū." ''Kodansha Encyclopedia of Japan.'' 18 December 2007 {{Imperial Waka Anthologies}} {{Authority control}} {{draft categories| [[Category: of the ''Kokinshū''. ==Editors of the anthology== Although Go-Toba retained veto power over the poems included in the anthology as well as the order in which they were presented,Kodansha ''Shin Kokinshū'' he assigned the task of compilation to six of the Fellows of the Bureau of Poetry.Brower 8 These were [[Fujiwara no Teika]] [[Category:Japanese poetry anthologies]] [[Category:1200s in Japan]] [[Category:Emperor Go-Toba]] [[Category:Buddhist poetry]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]] }} [[sn:Shin Kokin Wakashū]] [[en:Shin Kokin Wakashū]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[Category:pages with valid en interwikis]] [[ar:شين كوكين واكاشو]] {{Commons category|Shin Kokin Wakashū}} [[de:Shinkokin-wakashū]] [[fa:شین کوکین واکاشو]] [[fr:Shin kokin wakashū]] [[it:Shin Kokin Wakashū]] [[ja:新古今和歌集]] [[ko:신고금 와카집]] [[ru:Син кокин вака-сю]] [[uk:Сін кокін вака-сю]] [[zh:新古今和歌集]]

''Kodansha Encyclopedia of Japan.'' 18 December 2007 {{Imperial Waka Anthologies}} {{Authority control}} {{draft categories| [[Category: of the ''Kokinshū''. ==Editors of the anthology== Although Go-Toba retained veto power over the poems included in the anthology as well as the order in which they were presented,Kodansha ''Shin Kokinshū'' he assigned the task of compilation to six of the Fellows of the Bureau of Poetry.Brower 8 These were [[Fujiwara no Teika]] [[Category:Japanese poetry anthologies]] [[Category:1200s in Japan]] [[Category:Emperor Go-Toba]] [[Category:Buddhist poetry]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]] }} [[sn:Shin Kokin Wakashū]] [[en:Shin Kokin Wakashū]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[Category:pages with valid en interwikis]] [[ar:شين كوكين واكاشو]] {{Commons category|Shin Kokin Wakashū}} [[de:Shinkokin-wakashū]] [[fa:شین کوکین واکاشو]] [[fr:Shin kokin wakashū]] [[it:Shin Kokin Wakashū]] [[ja:新古今和歌集]] [[ko:신고금 와카집]] [[ru:Син кокин вака-сю]] [[uk:Сін кокін вака-сю]] [[zh:新古今和歌集]]

{{Short description|8th anthology of Japanese waka poetry (compiled 1201)}} {{italic title}} The {{nihongo|'''''Shin Kokin Wakashū'''''|新古今和歌集||"New Collection of Poems Ancient and Modern"}}, also known in abbreviated form as the {{nihongo|'''''Shin Kokinshū'''''|新古今集}} or even conversationally as the '''Shin Kokin''', is the eighth [[Nijūichidaishū|imperial anthology]] of [[waka (poetry)|waka]] poetry compiled by the Japanese court, beginning with the ''[[Kokin Wakashū]]'' circa 905 and ending with the ''[[Shinshokukokin Wakashū]]'' circa 1439. The name can be literally translated as "New Collection of Ancient and Modern Poems"Keene 192 and bears an intentional resemblance to that of the first anthology. Together with the ''[[Man'yōshū]]'' and the ''[[Kokin Wakashū|Kokinshū]]'', the ''Shin Kokinshū'' is widely considered to be one of the three most influential poetic anthologies in Japanese literary history. It was commissioned in 1201 by the [[:en:Daijō-tennō|retired emperor]] [[:en:Emperor Go-Toba|Go-Toba]] (r. 1183–1198), who established a new Bureau of Poetry at his Nijō palace with eleven Fellows,Brower 8 headed by [[Fujiwara no Yoshitsune]],Kodansha ''Fujiwara no Yoshitsune'' for the purpose of conducting poetry contests and compiling the anthology. Despite its emphasis on contemporary poets, the ''Shin Kokinshū'' covered a broader range of poetic ages than the ''Kokinshū'', including ancient poems that the editors of the first anthology had deliberately excluded.Cook Introduction It was officially presented in 1205, on the 300th anniversary of the Completion tier 2]] (1162–1241), [[:en:Fujiwara no Ariie|Fujiwara no Ariie]] (1155–1216), [[:en:Fujiwara no Ietaka|Fujiwara no Ietaka]] (1158–1237), [[Jakuren]] (c. 1139–1202), [[Minamoto no Michitomo]] (1171–1237) and [[:en:Asukai Masatsune|Asukai Masatsune]] (1170–1221). The anthology was also given a preface in Japanese prose by [[Fujiwara no Yoshitsune]] and a preface in Chinese—the scholarly language of the Court—by [[Fujiwara no Chikatsune]], in a manner reminiscent of the ''Kokinshū''. ==Significance== The significance of the ''Shin Kokinshū'' lies prominently in the technical expertise of the compilers, their novel and extensive use of the [[honkadori]] literary technique, and the effect that each of these elements had on Japanese poetry after its publication. Even though the ''Kokinshū'' is famous in part for its organization throughout the anthology, as each poem is generally given as a lead-in to the next, the ''Shin Kokinshū'' goes above and beyond the standard created by the original collection. The ''Kokinshū'' editors used linking poems as a general guide, but the ''Shin Kokinshū'' editors created "an anthology that may be read from beginning to end as a single long structure divided into books".Konishi 68 For example, in the sections on the topic of Spring, the editors pieced together a detailed representation in poetic form of the advancement of spring and the passage of time, using similar words and expressions to link each poem to the next.Konishi 74 In the section on travel poems, the progression is from ancient poets and styles to modern ones,Konishi 91 and as is common in Japanese waka anthologies, the sections on Love are arranged to show the stages of an affair from first love to bitter parting. This kind of detailed manipulation resulted in an anthology that did not necessarily contain all of the best works of the day. As Fujiwara no Teika complained, Go-Toba’s insistence on including the works of old, obscure or even unaccomplished composers in the anthology in order to maintain a11ropriate links to those poems that were worthwhile made the honor of having forty-six of his own poems included in the anthology less satisfactory.Brower 21 Individual egos aside, the result was a composition that not only spanned centuries of Japanese literary tradition and evolving literary styles but also provided a veritable textbook on what well and poorly written poems looked like. The elaborate linking format developed by the editors was also picked up and carried forward with the development of the [[renga]] or "linked verse" form, in which poets wrote a series of verses together in turns by continuing the image of the previous verse and introducing something new for the next poet to work with. Renga also made frequent use of the honkadori technique, since each poet had only a short phrase to work with and the ability to use allusions to prior, complete poems was an important one. The term "Honkadori" refers to the practice of "allusive variation," and can be literally translated as "taking from an original poem".Kodansha ''Honkadori'' Even though allusions to older poems were common in the poetic discourse of the day, following the 11th century and prior to Fujiwara no Teika’s experimentation with honkadori, it was frowned upon to make obvious borrowings from past writers.Bialock 182 However, that changed significantly with the publication of the ''Shin Kokinshū''. Instead of mimicking only the horizontal flow of the ''Kokinshū'', the poems in the New Collection also make vertical links to the poetic traditions of the past,Bialock 198 and by borrowing from specific poems and not simply from stock phrases, the authors and editors of the poems in the'' Shin Kokinshū'' were able to step away from overused and more clearly unoriginal topics that ancient poems had popularized.Bialock 208 The following example compares one of Teika’s own poems in the ''Shin Kokinshū'' to its honka, or original poem, in the ''Kokinshū''. {{Verse translation| {{lang|ja-Latn|Samushiro ya matsu yo no aki no kaze fukete tsuki wo katashiku uji no hashihime}} | How cold! waiting out the autumn’s weary night deepening as the wind blows she spreads out the moon’s light the Princess of Uji Bridge.|attr2=Teika SKKS 4:420Bialock 205}} {{Verse translation| {{lang|ja-Latn|Samushiro ni koromo katashiki koyoi mo ya ware wo matsuramu uji no hashihime}} | On the cold mat spreading out her gown, this night too will she wait for me— the Princess of Uji Bridge?|attr2=KKS 14:684Bialock 205}} Although the poems are written on the same subject, with the newer one drawing directly from the older, Fujiwara no Teika’s interpretation both modernizes the poem and provides it with greater subtlety. It also accomplishes a connection between the ''Kokinshū'' and the ''Shin Kokinshū''. ==Structure== The structure of the ''Shin Kokinshū'' echoes that of the ''Kokinshū'' in many ways, but it also shows the influence of the intervening imperial anthologies. As can be seen in the table below, the ''Shin Kokinshū'' omits certain books from the original anthology, and includes others on poetic topics that gained prominence only after the publication of the ''Kokinshū''. {| class="wikitable" |- ! Topic !colspan="2" align="center"| Kokinshū !colspan="2" align="center"| Shin Kokinshū |- !rowspan="4" valign="middle"| Seasons | 1-2 || Spring |align="right"|1-2||Spring |- | 3 || Summer |align="right"|3||Summer |- | 4-5 || Autumn |align="right"|4-5||Autumn |- | 6 || Winter |align="right"|6||Winter |- !rowspan="4"|   | 7 || Congratulations |align="right"| 7 || Congratulations |- | 8 || Partings |align="right"| 8 || Laments |- | 9 || Travel |align="right"| 9 || Partings |- | 10 || Acrostics |align="right"| 10 || Travel |- ! Love | 11-15 || Love |align="right"| 11-15 || Love |- !rowspan="4" valign="middle"| Miscellany | 16 || Laments !rowspan="2" valign="middle"| 16-18 !rowspan="2" valign="middle"| Miscellaneous |- | 17-18 || Miscellaneous |- | 19 || Miscellaneous Forms |align="right"| 19 || Shinto Poems |- |valign="top"| 20 | Traditional Poems
from the Bureau of Song |valign="top" align="right"| 20 |valign="top"| Buddhist Poems |} The 20 books of the Shin Kokinshū contain nearly 2,000 [[waka (poetry)|waka]], with the number varying depending on the edition, as Go-Toba continued to edit the anthology extensively even after his exile to the island of Oki.Cook Editorial Note Each poem is introduced with information regarding the occasion for which it was composed (if that information was available) and in most cases an author is also listed. Major contemporary poetic contributors to the Shin Kokinshū include [[Saigyō Hōshi|Saigyō]] with 94 poems; [[Jien]] with 92; Fujiwara no Yoshitsune with 79; [[:en:Fujiwara no Shunzei|Fujiwara no Shunzei]] with 72; [[Princess Shikishi]] with 49; Fujiwara no Teika with 46; Fujiwara no Ietaka with 43; Jakuren with 35; and Go-Toba with 33. ==See also== * [[:en:1205 in poetry|1205 in poetry]] * [[List of Japanese poetry anthologies]] * [[Japanese poetry]] * [[:en:13th century in poetry|13th century in poetry]] * [[Honkadori]] ==Notes== {{reflist}} ==References== *{{citation|last = Bialock|first = David T.|year = 1994|title = Voice, Text, and the Question of Poetic Borrowing in Late Classical Japanese Poetry|periodical = [[Harvard Journal of Asiatic Studies]]|volume = 54|pages = 181–231|jstor =2719391|doi = 10.2307/2719391|issue = 1|publisher = Harvard-Yenching Institute }} *{{citation|last = Brower|first = Robert H.|year = 1972|title = 'Ex-Emperor Go-Toba's Secret Teachings': Go-Toba no in Gokuden|periodical = [[Harvard Journal of Asiatic Studies]]|volume = 32|pages = 5–70|doi = 10.2307/2718867|jstor = 2718867|publisher = Harvard-Yenching Institute|url = https://dl.dropboxusercontent.com/u/182368464/japanese-literature/1972-brower-exemperorgotobassecretteachings.pdf|url-status = dead|archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20160303193542/https://dl.dropboxusercontent.com/u/182368464/japanese-literature/1972-brower-exemperorgotobassecretteachings.pdf|archive-date = 2016-03-03}} *{{citation|last = Cook|first = Lewis|date = August 1999 |at=Introduction|title = Shinkokinshū|periodical = Japanese Text Initiative|url = http://etext.virginia.edu/japanese/shinkokinshu/AnoShin.intro.html}} *{{citation|last = Cook|first = Lewis|date = August 1999 |at=Editorial Note|title = Shinkokinshū|periodical = Japanese Text Initiative|url = http://etext.virginia.edu/japanese/shinkokinshu/AnoShin.ednote.html}} *{{citation|last=Keene|first=Donald|author-link=Donald Keene|year=1955|title=Anthology of Japanese Literature|place= New York, NY|publisher= Grove Press}} *{{citation|last1 = Konishi|first1 = Jun'ichi|year = 1958 |last2 = Trans. Robert H. Brower and Earl Miner|title = Association and Progression: Principles of Integration in Anthologies and Sequences of Japanese Court Poetry, A.D. 900-1350|periodical = [[Harvard Journal of Asiatic Studies]]|volume = 21|pages = 67–127|doi = 10.2307/2718620|jstor = 2718620|author3 = Miner, Earl|publisher = Harvard-Yenching Institute|first2 = Robert H.}} *"Honkadori." ''Kodansha Encyclopedia of Japan.'' 18 December 2007 *"Fujiwara no Yoshitsune." ''Kodansha Encyclopedia of Japan.'' 18 December 2007 *"Shin Kokinshū." ''Kodansha Encyclopedia of Japan.'' 18 December 2007 {{Imperial Waka Anthologies}} {{Authority control}} {{draft categories| [[Category: of the ''Kokinshū''. ==Editors of the anthology== Although Go-Toba retained veto power over the poems included in the anthology as well as the order in which they were presented,Kodansha ''Shin Kokinshū'' he assigned the task of compilation to six of the Fellows of the Bureau of Poetry.Brower 8 These were [[Fujiwara no Teika]] [[Category:Japanese poetry anthologies]] [[Category:1200s in Japan]] [[Category:Emperor Go-Toba]] [[Category:Buddhist poetry]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]] }} [[sn:Shin Kokin Wakashū]] [[en:Shin Kokin Wakashū]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[Category:pages with valid en interwikis]] [[ar:شين كوكين واكاشو]] {{Commons category|Shin Kokin Wakashū}} [[de:Shinkokin-wakashū]] [[fa:شین کوکین واکاشو]] [[fr:Shin kokin wakashū]] [[it:Shin Kokin Wakashū]] [[ja:新古今和歌集]] [[ko:신고금 와카집]] [[ru:Син кокин вака-сю]] [[uk:Сін кокін вака-сю]] [[zh:新古今和歌集]]

It was commissioned in 1201 by the [[:en:Daijō-tennō|retired emperor]] [[:en:Emperor Go-Toba|Go-Toba]] (r. 1183–1198), who established a new Bureau of Poetry at his Nijō palace with eleven Fellows,Brower 8 headed by [[:en:Fujiwara no Yoshitsune|Fujiwara no Yoshitsune]],Kodansha ''Fujiwara no Yoshitsune'' for the purpose of conducting poetry contests and compiling the anthology. Despite its emphasis on contemporary poets, the ''Shin Kokinshū'' covered a broader range of poetic ages than the ''Kokinshū'', including ancient poems that the editors of the first anthology had deliberately excluded.Cook Introduction It was officially presented in 1205, on the 300th anniversary of the Completion tier 2]] (1162–1241), [[:en:Fujiwara no Ariie|Fujiwara no Ariie]] (1155–1216), [[:en:Fujiwara no Ietaka|Fujiwara no Ietaka]] (1158–1237), [[Jakuren]] (c. 1139–1202), [[Minamoto no Michitomo]] (1171–1237) and [[:en:Asukai Masatsune|Asukai Masatsune]] (1170–1221). The anthology was also given a preface in Japanese prose by [[:en:Fujiwara no Yoshitsune|Fujiwara no Yoshitsune]] and a preface in Chinese—the scholarly language of the Court—by [[Fujiwara no Chikatsune]], in a manner reminiscent of the ''Kokinshū''. ==Significance== The significance of the ''Shin Kokinshū'' lies prominently in the technical expertise of the compilers, their novel and extensive use of the [[honkadori]] literary technique, and the effect that each of these elements had on Japanese poetry after its publication. Even though the ''Kokinshū'' is famous in part for its organization throughout the anthology, as each poem is generally given as a lead-in to the next, the ''Shin Kokinshū'' goes above and beyond the standard created by the original collection. The ''Kokinshū'' editors used linking poems as a general guide, but the ''Shin Kokinshū'' editors created "an anthology that may be read from beginning to end as a single long structure divided into books".Konishi 68 For example, in the sections on the topic of Spring, the editors pieced together a detailed representation in poetic form of the advancement of spring and the passage of time, using similar words and expressions to link each poem to the next.Konishi 74 In the section on travel poems, the progression is from ancient poets and styles to modern ones,Konishi 91 and as is common in Japanese waka anthologies, the sections on Love are arranged to show the stages of an affair from first love to bitter parting. This kind of detailed manipulation resulted in an anthology that did not necessarily contain all of the best works of the day. As Fujiwara no Teika complained, Go-Toba’s insistence on including the works of old, obscure or even unaccomplished composers in the anthology in order to maintain a11ropriate links to those poems that were worthwhile made the honor of having forty-six of his own poems included in the anthology less satisfactory.Brower 21 Individual egos aside, the result was a composition that not only spanned centuries of Japanese literary tradition and evolving literary styles but also provided a veritable textbook on what well and poorly written poems looked like. The elaborate linking format developed by the editors was also picked up and carried forward with the development of the [[renga]] or "linked verse" form, in which poets wrote a series of verses together in turns by continuing the image of the previous verse and introducing something new for the next poet to work with. Renga also made frequent use of the honkadori technique, since each poet had only a short phrase to work with and the ability to use allusions to prior, complete poems was an important one. The term "Honkadori" refers to the practice of "allusive variation," and can be literally translated as "taking from an original poem".Kodansha ''Honkadori'' Even though allusions to older poems were common in the poetic discourse of the day, following the 11th century and prior to Fujiwara no Teika’s experimentation with honkadori, it was frowned upon to make obvious borrowings from past writers.Bialock 182 However, that changed significantly with the publication of the ''Shin Kokinshū''. Instead of mimicking only the horizontal flow of the ''Kokinshū'', the poems in the New Collection also make vertical links to the poetic traditions of the past,Bialock 198 and by borrowing from specific poems and not simply from stock phrases, the authors and editors of the poems in the'' Shin Kokinshū'' were able to step away from overused and more clearly unoriginal topics that ancient poems had popularized.Bialock 208 The following example compares one of Teika’s own poems in the ''Shin Kokinshū'' to its honka, or original poem, in the ''Kokinshū''. {{Verse translation| {{lang|ja-Latn|Samushiro ya matsu yo no aki no kaze fukete tsuki wo katashiku uji no hashihime}} | How cold! waiting out the autumn’s weary night deepening as the wind blows she spreads out the moon’s light the Princess of Uji Bridge.|attr2=Teika SKKS 4:420Bialock 205}} {{Verse translation| {{lang|ja-Latn|Samushiro ni koromo katashiki koyoi mo ya ware wo matsuramu uji no hashihime}} | On the cold mat spreading out her gown, this night too will she wait for me— the Princess of Uji Bridge?|attr2=KKS 14:684Bialock 205}} Although the poems are written on the same subject, with the newer one drawing directly from the older, Fujiwara no Teika’s interpretation both modernizes the poem and provides it with greater subtlety. It also accomplishes a connection between the ''Kokinshū'' and the ''Shin Kokinshū''. ==Structure== The structure of the ''Shin Kokinshū'' echoes that of the ''Kokinshū'' in many ways, but it also shows the influence of the intervening imperial anthologies. As can be seen in the table below, the ''Shin Kokinshū'' omits certain books from the original anthology, and includes others on poetic topics that gained prominence only after the publication of the ''Kokinshū''. {| class="wikitable" |- ! Topic !colspan="2" align="center"| Kokinshū !colspan="2" align="center"| Shin Kokinshū |- !rowspan="4" valign="middle"| Seasons | 1-2 || Spring |align="right"|1-2||Spring |- | 3 || Summer |align="right"|3||Summer |- | 4-5 || Autumn |align="right"|4-5||Autumn |- | 6 || Winter |align="right"|6||Winter |- !rowspan="4"|   | 7 || Congratulations |align="right"| 7 || Congratulations |- | 8 || Partings |align="right"| 8 || Laments |- | 9 || Travel |align="right"| 9 || Partings |- | 10 || Acrostics |align="right"| 10 || Travel |- ! Love | 11-15 || Love |align="right"| 11-15 || Love |- !rowspan="4" valign="middle"| Miscellany | 16 || Laments !rowspan="2" valign="middle"| 16-18 !rowspan="2" valign="middle"| Miscellaneous |- | 17-18 || Miscellaneous |- | 19 || Miscellaneous Forms |align="right"| 19 || Shinto Poems |- |valign="top"| 20 | Traditional Poems
from the Bureau of Song |valign="top" align="right"| 20 |valign="top"| Buddhist Poems |} The 20 books of the Shin Kokinshū contain nearly 2,000 [[waka (poetry)|waka]], with the number varying depending on the edition, as Go-Toba continued to edit the anthology extensively even after his exile to the island of Oki.Cook Editorial Note Each poem is introduced with information regarding the occasion for which it was composed (if that information was available) and in most cases an author is also listed. Major contemporary poetic contributors to the Shin Kokinshū include [[Saigyō Hōshi|Saigyō]] with 94 poems; [[Jien]] with 92; Fujiwara no Yoshitsune with 79; [[:en:Fujiwara no Shunzei|Fujiwara no Shunzei]] with 72; [[Princess Shikishi]] with 49; Fujiwara no Teika with 46; Fujiwara no Ietaka with 43; Jakuren with 35; and Go-Toba with 33. ==See also== * [[:en:1205 in poetry|1205 in poetry]] * [[List of Japanese poetry anthologies]] * [[Japanese poetry]] * [[:en:13th century in poetry|13th century in poetry]] * [[Honkadori]] ==Notes== {{reflist}} ==References== *{{citation|last = Bialock|first = David T.|year = 1994|title = Voice, Text, and the Question of Poetic Borrowing in Late Classical Japanese Poetry|periodical = [[Harvard Journal of Asiatic Studies]]|volume = 54|pages = 181–231|jstor =2719391|doi = 10.2307/2719391|issue = 1|publisher = Harvard-Yenching Institute }} *{{citation|last = Brower|first = Robert H.|year = 1972|title = 'Ex-Emperor Go-Toba's Secret Teachings': Go-Toba no in Gokuden|periodical = [[Harvard Journal of Asiatic Studies]]|volume = 32|pages = 5–70|doi = 10.2307/2718867|jstor = 2718867|publisher = Harvard-Yenching Institute|url = https://dl.dropboxusercontent.com/u/182368464/japanese-literature/1972-brower-exemperorgotobassecretteachings.pdf|url-status = dead|archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20160303193542/https://dl.dropboxusercontent.com/u/182368464/japanese-literature/1972-brower-exemperorgotobassecretteachings.pdf|archive-date = 2016-03-03}} *{{citation|last = Cook|first = Lewis|date = August 1999 |at=Introduction|title = Shinkokinshū|periodical = Japanese Text Initiative|url = http://etext.virginia.edu/japanese/shinkokinshu/AnoShin.intro.html}} *{{citation|last = Cook|first = Lewis|date = August 1999 |at=Editorial Note|title = Shinkokinshū|periodical = Japanese Text Initiative|url = http://etext.virginia.edu/japanese/shinkokinshu/AnoShin.ednote.html}} *{{citation|last=Keene|first=Donald|author-link=Donald Keene|year=1955|title=Anthology of Japanese Literature|place= New York, NY|publisher= Grove Press}} *{{citation|last1 = Konishi|first1 = Jun'ichi|year = 1958 |last2 = Trans. Robert H. Brower and Earl Miner|title = Association and Progression: Principles of Integration in Anthologies and Sequences of Japanese Court Poetry, A.D. 900-1350|periodical = [[Harvard Journal of Asiatic Studies]]|volume = 21|pages = 67–127|doi = 10.2307/2718620|jstor = 2718620|author3 = Miner, Earl|publisher = Harvard-Yenching Institute|first2 = Robert H.}} *"Honkadori." ''Kodansha Encyclopedia of Japan.'' 18 December 2007 *"Fujiwara no Yoshitsune." ''Kodansha Encyclopedia of Japan.'' 18 December 2007 *"Shin Kokinshū." ''Kodansha Encyclopedia of Japan.'' 18 December 2007 {{Imperial Waka Anthologies}} {{Authority control}} {{draft categories| [[Category: of the ''Kokinshū''. ==Editors of the anthology== Although Go-Toba retained veto power over the poems included in the anthology as well as the order in which they were presented,Kodansha ''Shin Kokinshū'' he assigned the task of compilation to six of the Fellows of the Bureau of Poetry.Brower 8 These were [[:en:Fujiwara no Teika|Fujiwara no Teika]] [[Category:Japanese poetry anthologies]] [[Category:1200s in Japan]] [[Category:Emperor Go-Toba]] [[Category:Buddhist poetry]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]] }} [[sn:Shin Kokin Wakashū]] [[en:Shin Kokin Wakashū]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[Category:pages with valid en interwikis]] [[ar:شين كوكين واكاشو]] {{Commons category|Shin Kokin Wakashū}} [[de:Shinkokin-wakashū]] [[fa:شین کوکین واکاشو]] [[fr:Shin kokin wakashū]] [[it:Shin Kokin Wakashū]] [[ja:新古今和歌集]] [[ko:신고금 와카집]] [[ru:Син кокин вака-сю]] [[uk:Сін кокін вака-сю]] [[zh:新古今和歌集]]

{{Short description|8th anthology of Japanese waka poetry (compiled 1201)}} {{italic title}} The {{nihongo|'''''Shin Kokin Wakashū'''''|新古今和歌集||"New Collection of Poems Ancient and Modern"}}, also known in abbreviated form as the {{nihongo|'''''Shin Kokinshū'''''|新古今集}} or even conversationally as the '''Shin Kokin''', is the eighth [[Nijūichidaishū|imperial anthology]] of [[waka (poetry)|waka]] poetry compiled by the Japanese court, beginning with the ''[[Kokin Wakashū]]'' circa 905 and ending with the ''[[Shinshokukokin Wakashū]]'' circa 1439. The name can be literally translated as "New Collection of Ancient and Modern Poems"Keene 192 and bears an intentional resemblance to that of the first anthology. Together with the ''[[Man'yōshū]]'' and the ''[[Kokin Wakashū|Kokinshū]]'', the ''Shin Kokinshū'' is widely considered to be one of the three most influential poetic anthologies in Japanese literary history. It was commissioned in 1201 by the [[:en:Daijō-tennō|retired emperor]] [[:en:Emperor Go-Toba|Go-Toba]] (r. 1183–1198), who established a new Bureau of Poetry at his Nijō palace with eleven Fellows,Brower 8 headed by [[:en:Fujiwara no Yoshitsune|Fujiwara no Yoshitsune]],Kodansha ''Fujiwara no Yoshitsune'' for the purpose of conducting poetry contests and compiling the anthology. Despite its emphasis on contemporary poets, the ''Shin Kokinshū'' covered a broader range of poetic ages than the ''Kokinshū'', including ancient poems that the editors of the first anthology had deliberately excluded.Cook Introduction It was officially presented in 1205, on the 300th anniversary of the Completion tier 2]] (1162–1241), [[:en:Fujiwara no Ariie|Fujiwara no Ariie]] (1155–1216), [[:en:Fujiwara no Ietaka|Fujiwara no Ietaka]] (1158–1237), [[Jakuren]] (c. 1139–1202), [[Minamoto no Michitomo]] (1171–1237) and [[:en:Asukai Masatsune|Asukai Masatsune]] (1170–1221). The anthology was also given a preface in Japanese prose by [[:en:Fujiwara no Yoshitsune|Fujiwara no Yoshitsune]] and a preface in Chinese—the scholarly language of the Court—by [[Fujiwara no Chikatsune]], in a manner reminiscent of the ''Kokinshū''. ==Significance== The significance of the ''Shin Kokinshū'' lies prominently in the technical expertise of the compilers, their novel and extensive use of the [[:en:Honkadori|Honkadori]] literary technique, and the effect that each of these elements had on Japanese poetry after its publication. Even though the ''Kokinshū'' is famous in part for its organization throughout the anthology, as each poem is generally given as a lead-in to the next, the ''Shin Kokinshū'' goes above and beyond the standard created by the original collection. The ''Kokinshū'' editors used linking poems as a general guide, but the ''Shin Kokinshū'' editors created "an anthology that may be read from beginning to end as a single long structure divided into books".Konishi 68 For example, in the sections on the topic of Spring, the editors pieced together a detailed representation in poetic form of the advancement of spring and the passage of time, using similar words and expressions to link each poem to the next.Konishi 74 In the section on travel poems, the progression is from ancient poets and styles to modern ones,Konishi 91 and as is common in Japanese waka anthologies, the sections on Love are arranged to show the stages of an affair from first love to bitter parting. This kind of detailed manipulation resulted in an anthology that did not necessarily contain all of the best works of the day. As Fujiwara no Teika complained, Go-Toba’s insistence on including the works of old, obscure or even unaccomplished composers in the anthology in order to maintain a11ropriate links to those poems that were worthwhile made the honor of having forty-six of his own poems included in the anthology less satisfactory.Brower 21 Individual egos aside, the result was a composition that not only spanned centuries of Japanese literary tradition and evolving literary styles but also provided a veritable textbook on what well and poorly written poems looked like. The elaborate linking format developed by the editors was also picked up and carried forward with the development of the [[renga]] or "linked verse" form, in which poets wrote a series of verses together in turns by continuing the image of the previous verse and introducing something new for the next poet to work with. Renga also made frequent use of the honkadori technique, since each poet had only a short phrase to work with and the ability to use allusions to prior, complete poems was an important one. The term "Honkadori" refers to the practice of "allusive variation," and can be literally translated as "taking from an original poem".Kodansha ''Honkadori'' Even though allusions to older poems were common in the poetic discourse of the day, following the 11th century and prior to Fujiwara no Teika’s experimentation with honkadori, it was frowned upon to make obvious borrowings from past writers.Bialock 182 However, that changed significantly with the publication of the ''Shin Kokinshū''. Instead of mimicking only the horizontal flow of the ''Kokinshū'', the poems in the New Collection also make vertical links to the poetic traditions of the past,Bialock 198 and by borrowing from specific poems and not simply from stock phrases, the authors and editors of the poems in the'' Shin Kokinshū'' were able to step away from overused and more clearly unoriginal topics that ancient poems had popularized.Bialock 208 The following example compares one of Teika’s own poems in the ''Shin Kokinshū'' to its honka, or original poem, in the ''Kokinshū''. {{Verse translation| {{lang|ja-Latn|Samushiro ya matsu yo no aki no kaze fukete tsuki wo katashiku uji no hashihime}} | How cold! waiting out the autumn’s weary night deepening as the wind blows she spreads out the moon’s light the Princess of Uji Bridge.|attr2=Teika SKKS 4:420Bialock 205}} {{Verse translation| {{lang|ja-Latn|Samushiro ni koromo katashiki koyoi mo ya ware wo matsuramu uji no hashihime}} | On the cold mat spreading out her gown, this night too will she wait for me— the Princess of Uji Bridge?|attr2=KKS 14:684Bialock 205}} Although the poems are written on the same subject, with the newer one drawing directly from the older, Fujiwara no Teika’s interpretation both modernizes the poem and provides it with greater subtlety. It also accomplishes a connection between the ''Kokinshū'' and the ''Shin Kokinshū''. ==Structure== The structure of the ''Shin Kokinshū'' echoes that of the ''Kokinshū'' in many ways, but it also shows the influence of the intervening imperial anthologies. As can be seen in the table below, the ''Shin Kokinshū'' omits certain books from the original anthology, and includes others on poetic topics that gained prominence only after the publication of the ''Kokinshū''. {| class="wikitable" |- ! Topic !colspan="2" align="center"| Kokinshū !colspan="2" align="center"| Shin Kokinshū |- !rowspan="4" valign="middle"| Seasons | 1-2 || Spring |align="right"|1-2||Spring |- | 3 || Summer |align="right"|3||Summer |- | 4-5 || Autumn |align="right"|4-5||Autumn |- | 6 || Winter |align="right"|6||Winter |- !rowspan="4"|   | 7 || Congratulations |align="right"| 7 || Congratulations |- | 8 || Partings |align="right"| 8 || Laments |- | 9 || Travel |align="right"| 9 || Partings |- | 10 || Acrostics |align="right"| 10 || Travel |- ! from the Bureau of Song |valign="top" align="right"| 20 |valign="top"| Buddhist Poems |} The 20 books of the Shin Kokinshū contain nearly 2,000 [[waka (poetry)|waka]], with the number varying depending on the edition, as Go-Toba continued to edit the anthology extensively even after his exile to the island of Oki.Cook Editorial Note Each poem is introduced with information regarding the occasion for which it was composed (if that information was available) and in most cases an author is also listed. Major contemporary poetic contributors to the Shin Kokinshū include [[Saigyō Hōshi|Saigyō]] with 94 poems; [[Jien]] with 92; Fujiwara no Yoshitsune with 79; [[:en:Fujiwara no Shunzei|Fujiwara no Shunzei]] with 72; [[Princess Shikishi]] with 49; Fujiwara no Teika with 46; Fujiwara no Ietaka with 43; Jakuren with 35; and Go-Toba with 33. ==See also== * [[:en:1205 in poetry|1205 in poetry]] * [[List of Japanese poetry anthologies]] * [[Japanese poetry]] * [[:en:13th century in poetry|13th century in poetry]] * [[:en:Honkadori|Honkadori]] ==Notes== {{reflist}} ==References== *{{citation|last = Bialock|first = David T.|year = 1994|title = Voice, Text, and the Question of Poetic Borrowing in Late Classical Japanese Poetry|periodical = [[Harvard Journal of Asiatic Studies]]|volume = 54|pages = 181–231|jstor =2719391|doi = 10.2307/2719391|issue = 1|publisher = Harvard-Yenching Institute }} *{{citation|last = Brower|first = Robert H.|year = 1972|title = 'Ex-Emperor Go-Toba's Secret Teachings': Go-Toba no in Gokuden|periodical = [[Harvard Journal of Asiatic Studies]]|volume = 32|pages = 5–70|doi = 10.2307/2718867|jstor = 2718867|publisher = Harvard-Yenching Institute|url = https://dl.dropboxusercontent.com/u/182368464/japanese-literature/1972-brower-exemperorgotobassecretteachings.pdf|url-status = dead|archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20160303193542/https://dl.dropboxusercontent.com/u/182368464/japanese-literature/1972-brower-exemperorgotobassecretteachings.pdf|archive-date = 2016-03-03}} *{{citation|last = Cook|first = Lewis|date = August 1999 |at=Introduction|title = Shinkokinshū|periodical = Japanese Text Initiative|url = http://etext.virginia.edu/japanese/shinkokinshu/AnoShin.intro.html}} *{{citation|last = Cook|first = Lewis|date = August 1999 |at=Editorial Note|title = Shinkokinshū|periodical = Japanese Text Initiative|url = http://etext.virginia.edu/japanese/shinkokinshu/AnoShin.ednote.html}} *{{citation|last=Keene|first=Donald|author-link=Donald Keene|year=1955|title=Anthology of Japanese Literature|place= New York, NY|publisher= Grove Press}} *{{citation|last1 = Konishi|first1 = Jun'ichi|year = 1958 |last2 = Trans. Instead of mimicking only the horizontal flow of the ''Kokinshū'', the poems in the New Collection also make vertical links to the poetic traditions of the past,Bialock 198 and by borrowing from specific poems and not simply from stock phrases, the authors and editors of the poems in the'' Shin Kokinshū'' were able to step away from overused and more clearly unoriginal topics that ancient poems had popularized.Bialock 208 The following example compares one of Teika’s own poems in the ''Shin Kokinshū'' to its honka, or original poem, in the ''Kokinshū''. {{Verse translation| {{lang|ja-Latn|Samushiro ya matsu yo no aki no kaze fukete tsuki wo katashiku uji no hashihime}} | How cold! waiting out the autumn’s weary night deepening as the wind blows she spreads out the moon’s light the Princess of Uji Bridge.|attr2=Teika SKKS 4:420Bialock 205}} {{Verse translation| {{lang|ja-Latn|Samushiro ni koromo katashiki koyoi mo ya ware wo matsuramu uji no hashihime}} | On the cold mat spreading out her gown, this night too will she wait for me— the Princess of Uji Bridge?|attr2=KKS 14:684Bialock 205}} Although the poems are written on the same subject, with the newer one drawing directly from the older, Fujiwara no Teika’s interpretation both modernizes the poem and provides it with greater subtlety. It also accomplishes a connection between the ''Kokinshū'' and the ''Shin Kokinshū''. ==Structure== The structure of the ''Shin Kokinshū'' echoes that of the ''Kokinshū'' in many ways, but it also shows the influence of the intervening imperial anthologies. As can be seen in the table below, the ''Shin Kokinshū'' omits certain books from the original anthology, and includes others on poetic topics that gained prominence only after the publication of the ''Kokinshū''. {| class="wikitable" |- ! Topic !colspan="2" align="center"| Kokinshū !colspan="2" align="center"| Shin Kokinshū |- !rowspan="4" valign="middle"| Seasons | 1-2 || Spring |align="right"|1-2||Spring |- | 3 || Summer |align="right"|3||Summer |- | 4-5 || Autumn |align="right"|4-5||Autumn |- | 6 || Winter |align="right"|6||Winter |- !rowspan="4"|   | 7 || Congratulations |align="right"| 7 || Congratulations |- | 8 || Partings |align="right"| 8 || Laments |- | 9 || Travel |align="right"| 9 || Partings |- | 10 || Acrostics |align="right"| 10 || Travel |- ! Love | 11-15 || Love |align="right"| 11-15 || Love |- !rowspan="4" valign="middle"| Miscellany | 16 || Laments !rowspan="2" valign="middle"| 16-18 !rowspan="2" valign="middle"| Miscellaneous |- | 17-18 || Miscellaneous |- | 19 || Miscellaneous Forms |align="right"| 19 || Shinto Poems |- |valign="top"| 20 | Traditional Poems
from the Bureau of Song |valign="top" align="right"| 20 |valign="top"| Buddhist Poems |} The 20 books of the Shin Kokinshū contain nearly 2,000 [[waka (poetry)|waka]], with the number varying depending on the edition, as Go-Toba continued to edit the anthology extensively even after his exile to the island of Oki.Cook Editorial Note Each poem is introduced with information regarding the occasion for which it was composed (if that information was available) and in most cases an author is also listed. Major contemporary poetic contributors to the Shin Kokinshū include [[Saigyō Hōshi|Saigyō]] with 94 poems; [[Jien]] with 92; Fujiwara no Yoshitsune with 79; [[:en:Fujiwara no Shunzei|Fujiwara no Shunzei]] with 72; [[Princess Shikishi]] with 49; Fujiwara no Teika with 46; Fujiwara no Ietaka with 43; Jakuren with 35; and Go-Toba with 33. ==See also== * [[:en:1205 in poetry|1205 in poetry]] * [[List of Japanese poetry anthologies]] * [[Japanese poetry]] * [[:en:13th century in poetry|13th century in poetry]] * [[:en:Honkadori|Honkadori]] ==Notes== {{reflist}} ==References== *{{citation|last = Bialock|first = David T.|year = 1994|title = Voice, Text, and the Question of Poetic Borrowing in Late Classical Japanese Poetry|periodical = [[Harvard Journal of Asiatic Studies]]|volume = 54|pages = 181–231|jstor =2719391|doi = 10.2307/2719391|issue = 1|publisher = Harvard-Yenching Institute }} *{{citation|last = Brower|first = Robert H.|year = 1972|title = 'Ex-Emperor Go-Toba's Secret Teachings': Go-Toba no in Gokuden|periodical = [[Harvard Journal of Asiatic Studies]]|volume = 32|pages = 5–70|doi = 10.2307/2718867|jstor = 2718867|publisher = Harvard-Yenching Institute|url = https://dl.dropboxusercontent.com/u/182368464/japanese-literature/1972-brower-exemperorgotobassecretteachings.pdf|url-status = dead|archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20160303193542/https://dl.dropboxusercontent.com/u/182368464/japanese-literature/1972-brower-exemperorgotobassecretteachings.pdf|archive-date = 2016-03-03}} *{{citation|last = Cook|first = Lewis|date = August 1999 |at=Introduction|title = Shinkokinshū|periodical = Japanese Text Initiative|url = http://etext.virginia.edu/japanese/shinkokinshu/AnoShin.intro.html}} *{{citation|last = Cook|first = Lewis|date = August 1999 |at=Editorial Note|title = Shinkokinshū|periodical = Japanese Text Initiative|url = http://etext.virginia.edu/japanese/shinkokinshu/AnoShin.ednote.html}} *{{citation|last=Keene|first=Donald|author-link=Donald Keene|year=1955|title=Anthology of Japanese Literature|place= New York, NY|publisher= Grove Press}} *{{citation|last1 = Konishi|first1 = Jun'ichi|year = 1958 |last2 = Trans. Robert H. Brower and Earl Miner|title = Association and Progression: Principles of Integration in Anthologies and Sequences of Japanese Court Poetry, A.D. 900-1350|periodical = [[Harvard Journal of Asiatic Studies]]|volume = 21|pages = 67–127|doi = 10.2307/2718620|jstor = 2718620|author3 = Miner, Earl|publisher = Harvard-Yenching Institute|first2 = Robert H.}} *"Honkadori." ''Kodansha Encyclopedia of Japan.'' 18 December 2007 *"Fujiwara no Yoshitsune." ''Kodansha Encyclopedia of Japan.'' 18 December 2007 *"Shin Kokinshū." ''Kodansha Encyclopedia of Japan.'' 18 December 2007 {{Imperial Waka Anthologies}} {{Authority control}} {{draft categories| [[Category: of the ''Kokinshū''. ==Editors of the anthology== Although Go-Toba retained veto power over the poems included in the anthology as well as the order in which they were presented,Kodansha ''Shin Kokinshū'' he assigned the task of compilation to six of the Fellows of the Bureau of Poetry.Brower 8 These were [[:en:Fujiwara no Teika|Fujiwara no Teika]] [[Category:Japanese poetry anthologies]] [[Category:1200s in Japan]] [[Category:Emperor Go-Toba]] [[Category:Buddhist poetry]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]] }} [[sn:Shin Kokin Wakashū]] [[en:Shin Kokin Wakashū]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[Category:pages with valid en interwikis]] [[ar:شين كوكين واكاشو]] {{Commons category|Shin Kokin Wakashū}} [[de:Shinkokin-wakashū]] [[fa:شین کوکین واکاشو]] [[fr:Shin kokin wakashū]] [[it:Shin Kokin Wakashū]] [[ja:新古今和歌集]] [[ko:신고금 와카집]] [[ru:Син кокин вака-сю]] [[uk:Сін кокін вака-сю]] [[zh:新古今和歌集]]

{{Short description|8th anthology of Japanese waka poetry (compiled 1201)}} {{italic title}} The {{nihongo|'''''Shin Kokin Wakashū'''''|新古今和歌集||"New Collection of Poems Ancient and Modern"}}, also known in abbreviated form as the {{nihongo|'''''Shin Kokinshū'''''|新古今集}} or even conversationally as the '''Shin Kokin''', is the eighth [[Nijūichidaishū|imperial anthology]] of [[waka (poetry)|waka]] poetry compiled by the Japanese court, beginning with the ''[[Kokin Wakashū]]'' circa 905 and ending with the ''[[Shinshokukokin Wakashū]]'' circa 1439. The name can be literally translated as "New Collection of Ancient and Modern Poems"Keene 192 and bears an intentional resemblance to that of the first anthology. Together with the ''[[Man'yōshū]]'' and the ''[[Kokin Wakashū|Kokinshū]]'', the ''Shin Kokinshū'' is widely considered to be one of the three most influential poetic anthologies in Japanese literary history. It was commissioned in 1201 by the [[:en:Daijō-tennō|retired emperor]] [[:en:Emperor Go-Toba|Go-Toba]] (r. 1183–1198), who established a new Bureau of Poetry at his Nijō palace with eleven Fellows,Brower 8 headed by [[:en:Fujiwara no Yoshitsune|Fujiwara no Yoshitsune]],Kodansha ''Fujiwara no Yoshitsune'' for the purpose of conducting poetry contests and compiling the anthology. Despite its emphasis on contemporary poets, the ''Shin Kokinshū'' covered a broader range of poetic ages than the ''Kokinshū'', including ancient poems that the editors of the first anthology had deliberately excluded.Cook Introduction It was officially presented in 1205, on the 300th anniversary of the Completion tier 2]] (1162–1241), [[:en:Fujiwara no Ariie|Fujiwara no Ariie]] (1155–1216), [[:en:Fujiwara no Ietaka|Fujiwara no Ietaka]] (1158–1237), [[:en:Jakuren|Jakuren]] (c. 1139–1202), [[Minamoto no Michitomo]] (1171–1237) and [[:en:Asukai Masatsune|Asukai Masatsune]] (1170–1221). The anthology was also given a preface in Japanese prose by [[:en:Fujiwara no Yoshitsune|Fujiwara no Yoshitsune]] and a preface in Chinese—the scholarly language of the Court—by [[Fujiwara no Chikatsune]], in a manner reminiscent of the ''Kokinshū''. ==Significance== The significance of the ''Shin Kokinshū'' lies prominently in the technical expertise of the compilers, their novel and extensive use of the [[:en:Honkadori|Honkadori]] literary technique, and the effect that each of these elements had on Japanese poetry after its publication. Even though the ''Kokinshū'' is famous in part for its organization throughout the anthology, as each poem is generally given as a lead-in to the next, the ''Shin Kokinshū'' goes above and beyond the standard created by the original collection. The ''Kokinshū'' editors used linking poems as a general guide, but the ''Shin Kokinshū'' editors created "an anthology that may be read from beginning to end as a single long structure divided into books".Konishi 68 For example, in the sections on the topic of Spring, the editors pieced together a detailed representation in poetic form of the advancement of spring and the passage of time, using similar words and expressions to link each poem to the next.Konishi 74 In the section on travel poems, the progression is from ancient poets and styles to modern ones,Konishi 91 and as is common in Japanese waka anthologies, the sections on Love are arranged to show the stages of an affair from first love to bitter parting. This kind of detailed manipulation resulted in an anthology that did not necessarily contain all of the best works of the day. As Fujiwara no Teika complained, Go-Toba’s insistence on including the works of old, obscure or even unaccomplished composers in the anthology in order to maintain a11ropriate links to those poems that were worthwhile made the honor of having forty-six of his own poems included in the anthology less satisfactory.Brower 21 Individual egos aside, the result was a composition that not only spanned centuries of Japanese literary tradition and evolving literary styles but also provided a veritable textbook on what well and poorly written poems looked like. The elaborate linking format developed by the editors was also picked up and carried forward with the development of the [[renga]] or "linked verse" form, in which poets wrote a series of verses together in turns by continuing the image of the previous verse and introducing something new for the next poet to work with. Renga also made frequent use of the honkadori technique, since each poet had only a short phrase to work with and the ability to use allusions to prior, complete poems was an important one. The term "Honkadori" refers to the practice of "allusive variation," and can be literally translated as "taking from an original poem".Kodansha ''Honkadori'' Even though allusions to older poems were common in the poetic discourse of the day, following the 11th century and prior to Fujiwara no Teika’s experimentation with honkadori, it was frowned upon to make obvious borrowings from past writers.Bialock 182 However, that changed significantly with the publication of the ''Shin Kokinshū''. Instead of mimicking only the horizontal flow of the ''Kokinshū'', the poems in the New Collection also make vertical links to the poetic traditions of the past,Bialock 198 and by borrowing from specific poems and not simply from stock phrases, the authors and editors of the poems in the'' Shin Kokinshū'' were able to step away from overused and more clearly unoriginal topics that ancient poems had popularized.Bialock 208 The following example compares one of Teika’s own poems in the ''Shin Kokinshū'' to its honka, or original poem, in the ''Kokinshū''. {{Verse translation| {{lang|ja-Latn|Samushiro ya matsu yo no aki no kaze fukete tsuki wo katashiku uji no hashihime}} | How cold! waiting out the autumn’s weary night deepening as the wind blows she spreads out the moon’s light the Princess of Uji Bridge.|attr2=Teika SKKS 4:420Bialock 205}} {{Verse translation| {{lang|ja-Latn|Samushiro ni koromo katashiki koyoi mo ya ware wo matsuramu uji no hashihime}} | On the cold mat spreading out her gown, this night too will she wait for me— the Princess of Uji Bridge?|attr2=KKS 14:684Bialock 205}} Although the poems are written on the same subject, with the newer one drawing directly from the older, Fujiwara no Teika’s interpretation both modernizes the poem and provides it with greater subtlety. It also accomplishes a connection between the ''Kokinshū'' and the ''Shin Kokinshū''. ==Structure== The structure of the ''Shin Kokinshū'' echoes that of the ''Kokinshū'' in many ways, but it also shows the influence of the intervening imperial anthologies. As can be seen in the table below, the ''Shin Kokinshū'' omits certain books from the original anthology, and includes others on poetic topics that gained prominence only after the publication of the ''Kokinshū''. {| class="wikitable" |- ! Topic !colspan="2" align="center"| Kokinshū !colspan="2" align="center"| Shin Kokinshū |- !rowspan="4" valign="middle"| Seasons | 1-2 || Spring |align="right"|1-2||Spring |- | 3 || Summer |align="right"|3||Summer |- | 4-5 || Autumn |align="right"|4-5||Autumn |- | 6 || Winter |align="right"|6||Winter |- !rowspan="4"|   | 7 || Congratulations |align="right"| 7 || Congratulations |- | 8 || Partings |align="right"| 8 || Laments |- | 9 || Travel |align="right"| 9 || Partings |- | 10 || Acrostics |align="right"| 10 || Travel |- ! Love | 11-15 || Love |align="right"| 11-15 || Love |- !rowspan="4" valign="middle"| Miscellany | 16 || Laments !rowspan="2" valign="middle"| 16-18 !rowspan="2" valign="middle"| Miscellaneous |- | 17-18 || Miscellaneous |- | 19 || Miscellaneous Forms |align="right"| 19 || Shinto Poems |- |valign="top"| 20 | Traditional Poems
from the Bureau of Song |valign="top" align="right"| 20 |valign="top"| Buddhist Poems |} The 20 books of the Shin Kokinshū contain nearly 2,000 [[waka (poetry)|waka]], with the number varying depending on the edition, as Go-Toba continued to edit the anthology extensively even after his exile to the island of Oki.Cook Editorial Note Each poem is introduced with information regarding the occasion for which it was composed (if that information was available) and in most cases an author is also listed. Major contemporary poetic contributors to the Shin Kokinshū include [[Saigyō Hōshi|Saigyō]] with 94 poems; [[Jien]] with 92; Fujiwara no Yoshitsune with 79; [[:en:Fujiwara no Shunzei|Fujiwara no Shunzei]] with 72; [[Princess Shikishi]] with 49; Fujiwara no Teika with 46; Fujiwara no Ietaka with 43; Jakuren with 35; and Go-Toba with 33. ==See also== * [[:en:1205 in poetry|1205 in poetry]] * [[List of Japanese poetry anthologies]] * [[:en:Japanese poetry|Japanese poetry]] * [[:en:13th century in poetry|13th century in poetry]] * [[:en:Honkadori|Honkadori]] ==Notes== {{reflist}} ==References== *{{citation|last = Bialock|first = David T.|year = 1994|title = Voice, Text, and the Question of Poetic Borrowing in Late Classical Japanese Poetry|periodical = [[Harvard Journal of Asiatic Studies]]|volume = 54|pages = 181–231|jstor =2719391|doi = 10.2307/2719391|issue = 1|publisher = Harvard-Yenching Institute }} *{{citation|last = Brower|first = Robert H.|year = 1972|title = 'Ex-Emperor Go-Toba's Secret Teachings': Go-Toba no in Gokuden|periodical = [[Harvard Journal of Asiatic Studies]]|volume = 32|pages = 5–70|doi = 10.2307/2718867|jstor = 2718867|publisher = Harvard-Yenching Institute|url = https://dl.dropboxusercontent.com/u/182368464/japanese-literature/1972-brower-exemperorgotobassecretteachings.pdf|url-status = dead|archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20160303193542/https://dl.dropboxusercontent.com/u/182368464/japanese-literature/1972-brower-exemperorgotobassecretteachings.pdf|archive-date = 2016-03-03}} *{{citation|last = Cook|first = Lewis|date = August 1999 |at=Introduction|title = Shinkokinshū|periodical = Japanese Text Initiative|url = http://etext.virginia.edu/japanese/shinkokinshu/AnoShin.intro.html}} *{{citation|last = Cook|first = Lewis|date = August 1999 |at=Editorial Note|title = Shinkokinshū|periodical = Japanese Text Initiative|url = http://etext.virginia.edu/japanese/shinkokinshu/AnoShin.ednote.html}} *{{citation|last=Keene|first=Donald|author-link=Donald Keene|year=1955|title=Anthology of Japanese Literature|place= New York, NY|publisher= Grove Press}} *{{citation|last1 = Konishi|first1 = Jun'ichi|year = 1958 |last2 = Trans. Robert H. Brower and Earl Miner|title = Association and Progression: Principles of Integration in Anthologies and Sequences of Japanese Court Poetry, A.D. 900-1350|periodical = [[Harvard Journal of Asiatic Studies]]|volume = 21|pages = 67–127|doi = 10.2307/2718620|jstor = 2718620|author3 = Miner, Earl|publisher = Harvard-Yenching Institute|first2 = Robert H.}} *"Honkadori." ''Kodansha Encyclopedia of Japan.'' 18 December 2007 *"Fujiwara no Yoshitsune." ''Kodansha Encyclopedia of Japan.'' 18 December 2007 *"Shin Kokinshū."
